coredata.dc:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for water content in ethanol and temperature sensing
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Xiangying
Cai, Lifen
He, Wei
Cao, Xuegong
Liu, Bin
Wang, Huaiqian
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, two tetrahydo[5]helicene-based dyes (THH1 and THH2) were studied on their twist intramolecular charge transfer. And the water-soluble blue carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized. Considering that their different optical propeties, the ratiometric fluorescence probes N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were constructed by mixing N-CDs and dyes simply. It was found that N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 could quickly and sensitively detect water content in ethanol, with linear range of 0.500–25.0 vol% and 0.500–30.0 vol%, respectively. What’s more, through the actual sample test, it showed that the detection had good accuracy and precision. At the same time, it was found that two ratiometric probes could also be applied to the thermometry with good reversibility based on optical temperature sensors.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Tetrahydro[5]helicene-based dyes
Carbon dots
Water content
Temperature

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Assay studies for water contents in organic solvents
Effect of temperature on ratiometric probes

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21275059, 21377124 and 21575044), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (No. 2015J01054, 2016J01062 and 2014J01048) and the Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001). Thanks a lot for the help from Dr. Chuan-Feng Chen and his students in Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Science, CAS Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Function for the support in THH synthesis.

This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21275059, 21377124 and 21575044), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (No. 2015J01054, 2016J01062 and 2014J01048) and the Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001). Thanks a lot for the help from Dr. Chuan-Feng Chen and his students in Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Science, CAS Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Function for the support in THH synthesis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image; inset: Particle size distribution of N-CDs; (b) Fluorescence (blue) and UV–Vis absorption (black) spectra of N-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Optimized geometric structures of THH1 and THH2.
Fluorescence spectra of (a) N-CDs/THH1 and (b) N-CDs/THH2; relationship curves between (c) I 430 nm /I 610 nm , (d) I 430 nm /I 555 nm and temperatures.

(a) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/THH1 in ethanol/water mixtures with different water content; (b) relationship curves between I 430 nm /I 610 nm and water content; (c) 1931CIE chromaticity diagram; (d) photos of N-CDs/THH1 under UV lamp at 365 nm.
Schematic illustration of the process of N-CDs/THH1 was applied for detecting water contents in ethanol and sensing temperature.
Frontier molecular orbital contours and percentage of contributions of THH1 and THH2.
Determination results of water content in samples.

head.ce:title: A novel ratiometric fluorescent probe for water content in ethanol and temperature sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions (HuaqiaoUniversity), Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Materials Science and Engineering
Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions (HuaqiaoUniversity)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Materials Science and Engineering, Key Laboratory of Molecular Designing and Green Conversions (HuaqiaoUniversity), Huaqiao University, Xiamen 361021, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Engineering, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Engineering, Huaqiao University, Quanzhou 362021, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The ratiometric fluorescence probes, which were constructed by mixing N-CDs and tetrahydo[5]helicene-based dyes with twist intramolecular charge transfer, could be used to detect water content in ethanol and temperature with visual effect.

In this paper, two tetrahydo[5]helicene-based dyes (THH1 and THH2) were studied on their twist intramolecular charge transfer. And the water-soluble blue carbon dots (N-CDs) were synthesized. Considering that their different optical propeties, the ratiometric fluorescence probes N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were constructed by mixing N-CDs and dyes simply. It was found that N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 could quickly and sensitively detect water content in ethanol, with linear range of 0.500–25.0 vol% and 0.500–30.0 vol%, respectively. What’s more, through the actual sample test, it showed that the detection had good accuracy and precision. At the same time, it was found that two ratiometric probes could also be applied to the thermometry with good reversibility based on optical temperature sensors.

ce:section.ce:para: It was common to find that trace water is mixed in ethanol. However water in ethanol sometimes causes some troubles, because it was detrimental to many organic reactions including their rates, product yields and selectivity [1,2]. Thus, it is very important to detect water content in ethanol before industrial productions had to process with ethanol. The fluorescent-based sensors which could be used to determine the water content in organic solvents have attracted great attention, because of their considerable advantages, such as low-cost of fabrication implementation, easy-operation, in situ rapid-detection, and high sensitivity [3,4].

In previous reports about these fluorescence sensors on water determinations, their mechanisms for fluorescence switching were usually related to covalent bond based on water or non-covalent modulation of photo-physical processes including water as a competitive ligand [5], photo induced electron transfer (PET) [6], intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) [7], excited state intramolecular proton transfer (ESIPT) [8] and so on. However, most of them on water detection performed only single signal with different PL intensity or wavelength, so them were sensitive to external conditions [9]. It was well-known that dual fluorescence ratiometric detection was one of the most effective methods that could eliminate the negative effects caused by the instrument and artificial errors. Therefore, such kinds of fluorescence probes were applied for detecting various analyte such as protein, small molecules, ions, and so on [10–12].

Recently, several kinds of ratiometric fluorescence probes had been reported to detect water content in organic solvents. Wang and co-workers [13] designed a coumarin conjugate, which had a linear ratiometric fluorescence response to water content in N, N-dimethylformamide and acetone. A ratiometric fluorescence probe by cladding carbon dots in the metal organic frame (MOF) was built by Dong [14]. The nanocomposites (Eu-MOFs/N, S-CDs) was employed as a dual-color composite probe for detecting water content in ethanol based on different responses to water content of Eu-MOFs and N, S-CDs.

Our group has also developed a series of ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on the different responses of the analyte towards two fluorophores. Most of them could be successfully employed for sensing ions or biomolecules with excellent LOD [15,16]. In general, the ratiometric fluorescence probe was constructed by two fluorophores, in which one fluorophores sensitive to target was linked with a fluorescence material which was inert or opposite response towards the target. Sometimes, two mechanisms would be involved in one ratiometric fluorescent sensor for detecting analyte [17]. Water usually showed negative influence towards organic fluorescence dyes with ICT. Thus, many organic dyes were used as water indicators in organic solvents.

Considering that tetrahydro[5]helicene-based dyes (THH1 and THH2), a class of organic dyes with intense full-color fluorescence emission and large Stokes shifts in both solution and solid states due to their combination of conjugative effect, bear strong intramolecular push-pull electronic effect and twist structure [18,19], they would exhibit twist intramolecular charge transfer (TICT), which caused compounds sensitive to solvent polarity. Therefore, THHI and THH2 (the structure is depicted in Fig. S1) might show excellent promise as fluorescence probes in water content determination.

In this paper, in order to accomplish a convenient and sensitive fluorescence ratiometric probe for detecting water content in ethanol, tetrahydro[5]helicene-based dye and carbon dots (N-CDs) with great emissive properties were required here. Moreover, carbon dots were selected because they had great deals of advantages including excellent optical properties, rich colors, low toxicity and easy preparation [20,21]. The ratiometric fluorescence probes N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were constructed by simply mixing two fluorescence materials. The obvious TICT processes of THH1 and THH2 were observed. Their fluorescence emission could be quenched by water but enhanced by high temperature. However, N-CDs were hydrophilic and sensitive to temperature because of aggregation-induced quenching. Therefore, N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 could be applied for detecting water contents in ethanol, and the ratiometric probes could detect water contents in real samples with high accuracies and good visualization effects. More importantly, they could be used as temperature ratiometric sensors with fast response and low noise (Scheme 1 ).

The N-CDs were prepared directly by a simple hydrothermal method, which has been described in a protocol reported by Yang et al. [22]. Briefly, 2 g of ammonium citrate was dissolved in 25 mL deionized water, and then the solution was heated at 190 °C in Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclaves for 6 h. After cooling down to room temperature naturally, dark green solutions were collected from ammonium citrate. The obtained crude product was further dialyzed over deionized water in a dialysis bag (retained molecular weight: 500 Da) with stirring constantly and recharging with deionized water to remove the raw materials. In the end, the as collected pure N-CDs solution was dried with a rotary evaporator to obtain dark N-CDs solid. Additionally, Experimental, materials, instruments, experimental process etc. could be seen on Supporting Information.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Assay studies for water contents in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of N-CDs/THH1 probe (0.350 mL) dissolved in each dry organic solvent was added to each Ethanol-Water mixture solution (3.65 mL) containing a series of concentrations of water (0–30.0 vol%). After the addition of probe and oscillating for 5 min, all fluorescence spectra were collected to each solution at 30 °C.

We carried out the N-CDs by a hydrothermal method and the TEM image of it examines that there were tiny ball-shaped nanoparticles with a uniform lateral size about 3.37 ± 0.660 nm (Fig. 1 a). Obviously, they could be dispersed in water automatically. As showed in Fig. S2a, XRD patterns of N-CDs showed wider and weaker (002) diffraction peaks centered at about 24.1° (interlayer spacing distance is 3.70 Å), which was in agreement with that of the bulk carbon structures [22]. In order to further characterize the chemical bonds and functional groups on the N-CDs, FTIR spectroscopy was carried out later. In Fig. S2b, FTIR spectra of N-CDs revealed that the compound possess clear amidogen: band at 3178 cm−1 was ascribed to N-H stretching vibration, and that at 1550 cm−1 ascribed to the deformation vibration of N-H; the band at 1655 cm−1 was corresponds to C=O stretching vibration, and that at 1396 cm−1 corresponds to the deformation vibration of C-N. Compared to the ammonium citrate, the characteristic of acyl structure was clearer, however, the ether group disappear, which could be signed at 1222 cm−1 and 1107 cm−1 [23]. The rich nitrogen and oxygen containing chemical groups might act as electron-donating group and could cause negative Zeta potential of the N-CDs. The N-CDs were indicated that they had negative Zeta potential at −44.8 mV. Because of the groups with negative Zeta potential, the strong hydrogen bonding between proton solvents and the N-CDs surface groups were allowable [24].

As showed in Fig. 1b, when the N-CDs were dissolved in ethanol, the UV–vis spectra displayed a broad absorption band below 238 nm and a sharp peak at 332 nm. The former one was ascribed to the π–π* transition of the small sp2-hybridized carbon clusters and the latter to the n–π* transition of the carbonyl bond, the main absorption band of fluorophores [25]. And the PL spectra of N-CDs exhibited the emission peaks at 440 nm at the excitation wavelength of 365 nm. The N-CDs exhibited excitation dependent emission, so its emission wavelength was tunable by selecting the optimally matched excitation [26] (Fig. S3). Therefore, N-CDs would be a great fluorophore to be used to construct ratiometric probe with other fluorescence materials.

Widely known, typical “D-π-A” type organic dyes with the electron donor/acceptors usually exhibited intramolecular charge transfer (ICT) effect, which made the compounds sensitive to micro polarity [27,28]. In the donor-tetrahydro[5]h- elicene-aceptor structural framing of two tetrahydro[5]helicene-based dyes THH1 and THH2, the thienyl and phenyl groups were effective electron donor while the carbonyl group belonging to imide served as the electron acceptor (Fig. 3). So it was suggested that THH1 and THH2 were “D-π-A” type organic dyes, and were sensitive to solvent polarity. Owing to the increasing solvent polarity, compounds with electron donor/acceptors might undergo significant red shift of emission peak and quenching of emission intensity. It also could be found that the PL spectra of THH1 and THH2 dissolving in different organic solvents showed red shifts respectively from non-polar solvent (eg. toluene) to polar solvent (eg. methanol) (Fig. S4). In addition, the orientation polarizability (Δf) of organic solvents and the solution optical properties of THH1 and THH2 were specifically listed in Table S1. With solvent polarity increasing (from toluene to methanol), evident declines in fluorescence quantum yields (THH1: from 56.3% to 0.500% and THH2: from 48.8% to 4.40%) and irregular changes in fluorescence lifetimes (τ) were observed. The results might be caused by the non-radiative transition between dye molecules and polar solvent molecules. It is worth to be noted that the quantum yield (QY) and τ of THH1 and THH2 in proton solvents (ethanol and methanol) were much less than them in other organic solvents, which might be caused not only by general polarity effects, but also by the hydrogen bonds interaction between carbonyl groups and proton solvents [13,29]. When THH1 and THH2 at excited states in proton solvents, the charge separation within the molecule was more obvious, at the same time, the oxygen atom became a stronger acceptor for hydrogen bonds, which reduced the energy of the excited state, leading to significant changes in emission characteristic including QY and τ. Thus, THH1 and THH2 might have ICT effects.

What's more, by Gaussian 09, we further calculated that the energy levels of the singlet and triplet states of THH1 and THH2. As showed in Fig. S5, the calculated results showed the energy gap of the S1 state and the T3 state of THH2 is only 0.0400 eV, which suggested S1 and T3 were almost identical. Such small singlet-tripletsplitting could provide a potential reverse intersystem crossing (RISC, T3 → S1) process, which was well-known as a “hot-exciton” process [32,33]. Furthermore, this energy gap was much smaller than the energy gap between T3 → T2 (0.340 eV) and T3 → T1 (0.580 eV). According to the energy gap law, the rate of T3 → T2 internal conversion (κIC) must be lower than the rate of RISC (κRISC). Therefore, the RISC process could quickly convert the high energy level triplet state to S1 and effectively avoid the non-radiative transition of T1 state, thereby improving QY of THH2 in non-proton polar solvent solvents. However, the minimum energy gap between the high-level triplet state and S1 of THH1was 0.0600 eV (T5 → S1), and the energy gap of T5 → T4 was 0.140 eV. So increasing the solvent polarity, the RISC process was difficult to occur in THH1. Therefore, it could be considered that THH1 was more sensitive to solvent polarity than THH2.

The ratiometric probe solution was constructed by mixing of dyes and N-CDs. When exciting at 340 nm, N-CDs/THH1 exhibited two well-resolved emission peaks at 430 nm and 610 nm, which originated from N-CDs and THH1, respectively. In addition, the emission peak at 555 nm of THH2 also could be indentified from 430 nm in N-CDs/THH2. To investigate the action mechanism between N-CDs and dyes, the concentration effects of them were checked. As shown in Fig. S6a and Fig. S6c, it was found that, when the concentration of N-CDs was fixed, with an increasing concentration of THH1 and THH2 the PL intensity of the N-CDs was gradually quenched. However, when the concentration of THH1 and THH2 were fixed, with an increasing concentration of N-CDs the PL intensity of two dyes were growing at the same time, as shown in Fig. S6b and Fig. S6d. Furthermore, the Zeta potential of THH1 and THH2 were tested. It could be clearly seen that the Zeta potential of THH1 and THH2 in ethanol were 23.5 mV and 10.1 mV, respectively (Fig. S7). Considering that the N-CDs negative Zeta potential. It suggested the N-CDs could connect with THH1 and THH2 by electrostatic adsorption. What’s more, the fluorescence lifetime decay kinetics for N-CDs, THH1 and THH2 alone or in the form of N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were determined by fitting the data to a double exponential decay. For N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2, strong quenching of N-CDs resulted in a shortening of its lifetime (Table S3) while the lifetimes of dyes were both increasing. These results indicated that energy transfer from N-CDs to THH1 or THH2 might occur.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of temperature on ratiometric probes
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate the stability of two ratiometric probes N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2, a constant thermostatic bath was used to control the temperature of solutions during the spectral test. As shown in Fig. 8a, the ratio of the intensity of two peaks (I 430 nm /I 610 nm and I 430 nm /I 555 nm ) in N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 solutions decreased with increasing temperature. It was interesting to find that the PL intensity of N-CDs was quenched by high temperatures, while the higher temperatures were benefit for the fluorescence emission of THH1 and THH2 (Fig. S8b). Compared to THH1, PL intensity of THH2 increased regularly and apparently, which might be caused by “hot exciton” process. The effect of temperatures on carbon dots had been studied by dynamic light scattering (DLS) to measure the N-CDs size distributions with different temperatures. The particle diameters of N-CDs in ethanol were gradually increasing when temperature was raised, as shown in Fig. S9. Therefore, it was suggested that N-CDs might undergo aggregation-induced quenching because of high temperatures [34]. Unlike N-CDs, the effect of temperature on THH1 and THH2 might be related to their TICT property. It was well-known that the fluorophores in TICT state were sensitive to temperature. The curves of fluorescence lifetimes of THH1 and THH2 in ethanol with different temperatures were collected (Fig. S10). It showed that the fluorescence lifetimes of THH1 and THH2 were increasing when temperatures was raised, which were in accord with other compounds with TICT. As temperature was raised, the higher energy level of the vibration band became active in TICT state, so the chances of electrons back to the ground states through radiant transition increased, resulting in increasing lifetimes and enhancing PL intensity. Thus, the ratiometric probes here were affected by temperatures in two aspects, but I 430 nm /I 610 nm and I 430 nm /I 555 nm in each probe decreased in the end. However, it was noteworthy that the ratio of the intensity of two peaks remained stable for 1 h when the temperature was controlled at 30 °C, as shown in Fig. S11.

As above mentions, the ratiometric probes we studied about were stable ethanol at one temperature. What's more interesting were the response of probes to different temperatures. As shown in Fig. 3 a and Fig. 3b, we measured the temperature-dependent emission spectra of N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 in ethanol from 5 to 65 °C in closed vessels. The emission intensity of N-CDs exhibited thermal dependence with decreasing, whereas the emission intensity of dyes was enhanced.

Interestingly, I 430 nm /I 610 nm and I 430 nm /I 555 nm were linearly decreased as the temperature raising, and their coefficients of correlation (R2 ) were even up 0.9989 for N-CDs/THH1 and 0.9935 for N-CDs/THH2 in the range of 5–65 °C (Fig. 3c and Fig. 3d). Therefore, they could function as ratiometric fluorescence sensing for temperature.

Moreover, stabilities of the probes were important parameter, which should be investigated to build temperature sensor [35]. To evaluate their reversibility as ratiometric probes for thermometer applications, the luminescent switching operation of N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 between 5 and 65 °C could be repeated severally for more than five consecutive cycles, which could be seen in Fig. 3e and Fig. 3f.

Considering that N-CDs exhibited an excellent hydrophilic and two tetrahydro[5]helicene derivatives exhibited TICT, which render the ratiometric probes were sensitive to solvent polarity[29]. We firstly examined the effect of water content (vol %) on the emission intensity of N-CDs and THH1 in ethanol, separately. As shown in Fig. S12a and S12b, the addition of water represented an enhancing effect on the emission intensity of N-CDs without linear relationship, while the addition of water to THH1 solution resulted in strongly quenching effect on the emission intensity with a linear decline when water content was 1.00–10.0 vol%. In addition, it could be seen in Fig. S12c and S12d, the 1931CIE chromaticity diagrams (1931CIE chromaticity diagram was proposed by International Commission on Illumination for the purpose of seeing the color relationship in a diagram easily) of N-CDs and THH1 indicated that the fluorescence colors of N-CDs hadn’t almost obvious changes. On the other hand, we found that the ratiometric probe (N-CDs/THH1) was significantly sensitive to water content. It probably was the effect of energy transfer between N-CDs and THH1, since the fluorescence of THH1 was quenched as adding water, which could recover the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs. Therefore, it could be indicated that the ratiometric probe could effectively improve the sensitivity of water detection.

N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 probes were dispersed in a series of ethanol solutions containing different water contents by oscillating at 30 °C, respectively. Then, the fluorescence spectra of two ratiometric probes were shown in Fig. 4 a. Upon gradually the water content in ethanol increased, it could be seen that the blue emission of N-CDs (I 430nm ) enhanced within a narrow range. Contrarily, the intensity of the emission peak of THH1 (I 610nm ) in N-CDs/THH1 decreased badly because of adding water. Simultaneously, the emission peak positions of THH1 showed a bit bathochromic shifts, which attributed to strong solvent-polarity dependence by TICT. For N-CDs/THH2, the emission intensity of I 555nm belonging to THH2 also was quenched (Fig. S13a). From our experiment, the PL intensity of I 430nm in single N-CDs mixing solutions were not increasing linearly with the water contents increasing in the system. However, I 430 nm /I 610 nm exhibited a well positive linear correlation (R2 = 0.9987) to the water contents in the range of 0.500–25.0 vol%, and the limit of detection (LOD) was 0.26% (Fig. 4b). Comparing to the linear curve of water contents and PL intensity in single THH1 solutions, the linear range was broadened and LOD reduced. These confirmed that the ratiometric probe would improve the accuracy of water detection level in ethanol. And for N-CDs/THH2, I 430 nm /I 555 nm showed a linear correlation (R2 = 0.9944) to the water contents from 1.00 vol% to 30.0 vol%, and the LOD was 0.33% (Fig. S13b).

In addition, 1931CIE chromaticity diagrams performed the color changes of N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 in ethanol with different water contents. In Fig. 4c, it symbolized for the colors of N-CDs/THH1 solution changing from pink to blue gradually as water content increasing. Because of increasing water content, the colors of N-CDs/THH2 solution changed from apricot to pink regularly (Fig. S13c). Furthermore, under the excitation of 365 nm UV light, the variation tendencies of colors of two ratiometric probes containing different water contents could be observed by naked eyes, as shown in Fig. 4d and Fig. S13d. Therefore, it suggested that two ratiometric fluorescence probes were both suitable for visual detection of water in ethanol at a wider range.

In order to investigate the accuracy of the established ratiometric fluorescence probes for detecting water contents in ethanol, three commercially available samples of 95% ethanol from different manufacturers was used. The water contents of the samples could be regarded as 5.00 vol%. The N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 probes were separately dissolved in them and the water contents were determined subsequently. The results are shown in Table 2 . It could be seen from the table that the recoveries of the two probes were between 95.0% and 105%, and their relative standard deviations (RSD) were all less than 3.00%, indicating that the ratiometric fluorescence probes had good accuracies and precision. Thus, N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 probes could be applied to determine the water content in real ethanol samples.

At the same time, we tested the water contents in methanol, N, N-dimethyl formamide (DMF) and dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) by using N-CDs/THH1,and found certain quantitative relationship between water contents and fluorescence strength (Fig. S14). Them might proved that the ratiometric probe was sensitive to solvent polarity.

In this paper, based on the hydrophilism of N-CDs and TICT of dye molecules, N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 could be applied for detecting water contents in ethanol. Compared with single fluorescent probes, the ratiometric probes had wide linear range and high sensitivity with good visualization effects. It could be obtained that the detection ranges of N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were 0.50–25.0 vol% and 0.50–30.0 vol%, respectively. In addition, it proved that both probes had good accuracy and precision in real samples. More importantly, it also found that N-CDs/THH1 and N-CDs/THH2 were applied to ratiometric sensing for temperature with excellent reversibility.

Xiangying Sun: Conceptualization, Formal analysis, Funding acquisition, Project administration, Resources, Supervision, Writing - review & editing. Lifen Cai: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Software, Validation, Visualization, Writing - original draft. Wei He: Investigation, Writing - review & editing. Xuegong Cao: Software. Bin Liu: Project administration. Huaiqian Wang: Software.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by the Natural Science Foundation of China (No.21275059, 21377124 and 21575044), the Natural Science Foundation of Fujian Province (No. 2015J01054, 2016J01062 and 2014J01048) and the Foundation of Graphene Powder & Composite Research Center of Fujian Province (2017H2001). Thanks a lot for the help from Dr. Chuan-Feng Chen and his students in Beijing National Laboratory for Molecular Science, CAS Key Laboratory of Molecular Recognition and Function for the support in THH synthesis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2021.120266.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A dual-emission probe to detect moisture and water in organic solvents based on green-Tb(3+) post-coordinated metal-organic frameworks with red carbon dots
Dalton T.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.X. Wu, B. Yan, A dual-emission probe to detect moisture and water in organic solvents based on green-Tb(3+) post-coordinated metal-organic frameworks with red carbon dots, Dalton T. 46 (2017) 7098.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bonding-induced emission of silyl-protected copper nanoclusters for luminescence turn-on detection of trace water in organic solvents
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Zhao, H. Feng, X. Zhang, H. Ao, Z. Qian, Bonding-induced emission of silyl-protected copper nanoclusters for luminescence turn-on detection of trace water in organic solvents, Analyst 142 (2017) 4613-4617.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A BODIPY sensor for water based on a photo-induced electron transfer method with fluorescence enhancement and attenuation systems
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Ooyama, M. Hato, T. Enoki, S. Aoyama, K. Furue, N. Tsunoji, J. Ohshita, A BODIPY sensor for water based on a photo-induced electron transfer method with fluorescence enhancement and attenuation systems, New J. Chem. 40 (2016) 7278-7281.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A novel cascade strategy with supramolecular and chemodosimetric methods for designing a fluorescent ratiometric detector hypersensitive to trace water
Analyst

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: G. Men, C. Chen, C. Liang, W. Han, S. Jiang, A novel cascade strategy with supramolecular and chemodosimetric methods for designing a fluorescent ratiometric detector hypersensitive to trace water, Analyst 140 (2015) 5454-5458.

Eur. J. Inorg. Chem.
